name: Particle IoT Platform
what_it_does: Particle's cloud platform provides REST APIs for managing IoT device fleets, reading sensor data, executing remote functions, and publishing/subscribing to device events.
use_cases: Remotely calling functions on Particle-connected microcontrollers from an agent
Reading real-time and historical sensor data from device fleets
Managing firmware over-the-air (OTA) updates across device groups
Subscribing to device event streams for reactive automation
Monitoring device online/offline status and diagnostics
not_for: Non-Particle hardware (API is tightly coupled to Particle device ecosystem)
High-frequency telemetry at sub-second intervals (rate limits apply)
General-purpose cloud hosting or non-IoT workloads
best_when: An agent needs to interact with Particle-based microcontroller deployments — reading sensors, triggering actuators, or managing firmware remotely.
avoid_when: You are using non-Particle hardware or need vendor-neutral IoT device management across mixed hardware fleets.

auth.notes: OAuth2 with user-level and product-level access tokens. Device-level tokens available for scoped access. Token scopes include read/write/admin per resource type.

pricing.notes: Free tier covers hobbyist use up to 100 devices. Paid tiers add SLA guarantees, higher data operation limits, and enterprise support.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST errors return JSON with error and error_description fields, but error codes are not always consistent across endpoints. Some errors surface hardware-layer messages that are opaque to API consumers.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency keys. Device function calls and variable reads are inherently stateful and tied to physical hardware; duplicate calls may trigger real-world actuator actions.
known_agent_gotchas: Calling a device function (POST /v1/devices/{id}/{function}) triggers a real-world action — agents must confirm intent before retrying
Device may be offline — API returns 408 timeout; agents need offline-handling logic
Access tokens expire; agents must handle token refresh proactively
Rate limits are per-product and per-user; shared keys across agent instances will collide
Event stream (SSE) requires persistent HTTP connection — not suitable for stateless agent patterns

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced on all endpoints. OAuth2 with scoped tokens is solid. However, device-level tokens with broad access are common in practice. No RBAC beyond basic scopes. No IP allowlisting on free tier.
